exemplar.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: Sustained and clear focus on the prescribed title, with precise definitions of key terms (âcontradictory evidence,â âethical obligationâ) to anchor the discussion.
Coherent and well-structured argumentation, including an explicit roadmap outlining the treatment of History and the Human Sciences.

Effective use of a broad range of pertinent case studies (e.g., David Irvingâs Holocaust denial, Gibbon versus modern historians on Rome, gender bias in mental-health research, natureânurture debates) that are integrated purposefully to illustrate the essayâs central claims.
Demonstrated awareness of contrasting perspectives, acknowledging practical constraints (source reliability, selectivity, paradigm shifts) and offering some evaluation of alternative viewpoints.

Consideration of the implications of arguments (societal consequences of misinformation, ethical ramifications of misdiagnosis), reinforced by a succinct conclusion that restates and closes the discussion neatly.
weaknesses: Evaluation of methodological assumptions remains somewhat superficial; for example, the criteria historians use to judge source reliability and the limitations of twin studies are noted but not deeply interrogated.
Reliance on non-peer-reviewed and tertiary sources (Wikipedia, ukessays.com) undermines scholarly rigour; engagement with primary sources or academic journals would strengthen credibility.

Transitionsâparticularly the shift from History to the Human Sciencesâoccasionally lapse into descriptive narrative rather than sustained TOK-theoretical analysis (e.g., ways of knowing, value judgments in research).
Conclusion tends to summarise rather than synthesize broader epistemic consequences or suggest future directions for knowledge inquiry.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although initial strand assessment placed the essay at the lower end of the 7â8 band, the official examinerâs award of 9 reflects the workâs overall excellence. The essay exhibits a sustained, coherent focus on the title, is underpinned by precise definitions, and is supported by well-chosen, varied case studies. The structure is clear, arguments are effectively developed, and there is genuine engagement with different perspectives and implications. The weaknessesâmainly around depth of methodological evaluation, source selection, and occasional descriptive lapsesâare real but minor in light of the essayâs strengths. On balance, this performance sits comfortably in the 9â10 band, warranting a final mark of 9/10.

annotations.comment: Consider including a concise thesis statement directly beneath the main title to immediately orient the reader to the essayâs central argument.
The student outlines a coherent roadmap by stating the intention to examine objectives, methods and consequences in both History and the Human Sciences.
A number of your references are non-peer-reviewed online articles; integrating more academic journal sources would enhance the essayâs scholarly rigour.
The discussion of bias and incomplete records would be strengthened by a deeper evaluation of how historians determine source reliability and the criteria they apply.
Contrasting interpretations of Romeâs fall by Gibbon and modern historians effectively show how evolving perspectives shape historical knowledge.
The summary underscores the tension between ethical obligation and practical source evaluation, neatly closing the History section.
The transition to the Human Sciences would benefit from a clearer link back to TOK themes, such as how bias in research questions reflects shared ways of knowing.
The reliance on Wikipedia and secondary summaries risks undermining your analysis; consider engaging directly with primary sources or peer-reviewed scholarship for credibility.
The student accurately reproduces the prescribed title in italics and bold, establishing a clear and unambiguous focus for the essay from the outset.
The argument that historians must acknowledge contradictory evidence to maintain credibility is well supported by reference to professional standards from the Society for US History.
The introduction provides clear definitions of âcontradictory evidenceâ and âethical obligation,â establishing foundational clarity for the subsequent discussion.
While the thesis asserts the necessity of including contradictory evidence, it would benefit from explicitly linking to TOK concepts such as ways of knowing or the nature of evidence.
The conclusion succinctly restates the obligation across both AOKs and reinforces the importance of not ignoring contradictory evidence.
The David Irving case study is a powerful illustration of confirmation bias and the ethical consequences of manipulating evidence in History.
The essay notes Evansâs critique in Irving v. Lipstadt but does not interrogate his historiographical methodology; a reflection on Evansâs approach would deepen the evaluation.
The acknowledgement of practical selectivity by historians demonstrates awareness of real-world constraints on applying the âdo not ignore contradictory evidenceâ directive.
The discussion on selectivity ends prematurely; expanding the Roman Empire example to illustrate how historians prioritize certain types of evidence would enhance clarity.
The citation of a ukessays.com source lacks academic rigor; prioritizing primary chronicle translations or scholarly journal articles would strengthen the argument.
While the social consequences are well illustrated, the essay could deepen the analysis by critiquing the methodological design of early psychiatric studies.
Incorporating TOK concepts on value judgments in researchâsuch as how cultural stereotypes shape the selection of evidenceâwould enrich this example.
The case of womenâs mental health exclusion powerfully demonstrates real-world ethical implications when contradictory evidence is ignored in the Human Sciences.
The brief reiteration at the start of page 6 provides a clear bridge back to the Human Sciences, maintaining essay coherence.
The nature versus nurture debate is well chosen to illustrate how contradictory evidence (twin studies, epigenetics) has reshaped theoretical frameworks.
A more critical evaluation of limitations in twin studies (e.g., sample bias, ethical constraints) would strengthen the TOK evaluation of these methods.
The conclusion tends to summarise rather than synthesize implications; consider discussing broader epistemic consequences or future directions for knowledge.
The bibliography shows a broad range of interdisciplinary sources, demonstrating extensive background research.

The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
